---
title: "Class 13: Transcriptomics and the analy sis of RNA-Seq data"
author: "Matthew Huang (A17978767)"
format: pdf
toc: true
---

## Background

We will perform an RNAseq analysis on the effects of dexamethasone ("dex"), a common steroid on airway smooth muscle (ASM) cell lines. 

## Data Import

We need two things for this analysis.

- **countData**: a table with genes as rows and samples/experiments as columns
- **colData**: metadata about the columns( ie samples) in the main countData object


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
counts <- read.csv("airway_scaledcounts.csv", row.names=1)
metadata <- read.csv("airway_metadata.csv") 

head(counts)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                SRR1039508 SRR1039509 SRR1039512 SRR1039513 SRR1039516
ENSG00000000003        723        486        904        445       1170
ENSG00000000005          0          0          0          0          0
ENSG00000000419        467        523        616        371        582
ENSG00000000457        347        258        364        237        318
ENSG00000000460         96         81         73         66        118
ENSG00000000938          0          0          1          0          2
                SRR1039517 SRR1039520 SRR1039521
ENSG00000000003       1097        806        604
ENSG00000000005          0          0          0
ENSG00000000419        781        417        509
ENSG00000000457        447        330        324
ENSG00000000460         94        102         74
ENSG00000000938          0          0          0
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
head(metadata)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
          id     dex celltype     geo_id
1 SRR1039508 control   N61311 GSM1275862
2 SRR1039509 treated   N61311 GSM1275863
3 SRR1039512 control  N052611 GSM1275866
4 SRR1039513 treated  N052611 GSM1275867
5 SRR1039516 control  N080611 GSM1275870
6 SRR1039517 treated  N080611 GSM1275871
```


:::
:::


## Check metadata counts correspondance

We need to check that the metadata matches the samples in our count data. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ncol(counts) == nrow(metadata)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] TRUE
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
colnames(counts) == metadata$id
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE
```


:::
:::


> Q1. How many genes are in this dataset?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nrow(counts)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 38694
```


:::
:::


> Q2. How many "control" cell lines do we have in the dataset?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sum(metadata$dex == "control")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 4
```


:::
:::


## Analysis Plan

> Q3.

We have 4 replicates per condition ("control" and "treated")
We want to compare the control vs the treated to see which genes expression levels change when we have the drug present. 

We will go row by row gene by gene) and see if the average value in control columns is different than the average value in treated columns

- Step 1. Find which columns in `counts` correspond to "control" samples. 

- Step 2. Extract these columns

- Step 3. Calculate an average value for each gene (e.g. each row). 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
control.inds <- metadata$dex == "control"
control.counts <- counts[, control.inds, drop = FALSE]
control.mean <- rowMeans(control.counts, na.rm = TRUE)
```
:::


>Q4 Do the same for "treated" samples - find the mean count value per gene. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
treated.inds <- metadata$dex == "treated"
treated.counts <- counts[, treated.inds, drop = FALSE]
treated.mean <- rowMeans(treated.counts, na.rm = TRUE)
```
:::


Lets put these two mean values into a new dataframe `meancounts` for easy book keeping and plotting.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
meancounts <- data.frame(
  control.mean = control.mean,
  treated.mean = treated.mean
)

head(meancounts)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                control.mean treated.mean
ENSG00000000003       900.75       658.00
ENSG00000000005         0.00         0.00
ENSG00000000419       520.50       546.00
ENSG00000000457       339.75       316.50
ENSG00000000460        97.25        78.75
ENSG00000000938         0.75         0.00
```


:::
:::


> Q5. Make a `ggplot` of average counts of control vs treated.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(meancounts, aes(x = control.mean, y = treated.mean)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class13_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-7-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::

> Q6. Log Transform

Itâ€™s better to log transform because counts are highly skewed.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(meancounts, aes(x = control.mean, y = treated.mean)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in scale_x_log10(): log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in scale_y_log10(): log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class13_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-8-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


## Log 2 units and fold change

If we consider "treated" / "control" counts we will get a number that tells us the change


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
log2(20 / 20)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# A doubling in treated versus control
log2(40 / 20)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 1
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
# A halving in treated versus control
log2(10 / 20)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] -1
```


:::
:::


> Add a new column `log2fc` for log2 fold change of treated/ control to our `meancounts` object.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
meancounts$log2fc <- log2(meancounts$treated.mean / meancounts$control.mean)
head(meancounts)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                control.mean treated.mean      log2fc
ENSG00000000003       900.75       658.00 -0.45303916
ENSG00000000005         0.00         0.00         NaN
ENSG00000000419       520.50       546.00  0.06900279
ENSG00000000457       339.75       316.50 -0.10226805
ENSG00000000460        97.25        78.75 -0.30441833
ENSG00000000938         0.75         0.00        -Inf
```


:::
:::


## Remove zero count genes

Typically we would not consider zero count genes - as we have no data about them and they should be excluded from further consideration. These lead to "funky" log2 fold change values (eg divide by zero errors etc.)

## DESeq analysis

We are missing any measure of significance from the work we have so far. Let's do this properly with the DESeq2 package


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(DESeq2)
```
:::


The DESeq2 package like many bioconductor packages, wants it's input in a very specific way -  a data structure setup with all the info it needs for the calculation. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts,
  colData = metadata,
  design = ~ dex
)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
converting counts to integer mode
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in DESeqDataSet(se, design = design, ignoreRank): some variables in
design formula are characters, converting to factors
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
dds <- dds[rowSums(counts(dds)) > 0, ]
```
:::


The main function in this package is called `DESeq()` it will run the full analysis for us on our `dds` input object:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
dds <- DESeq(dds)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
estimating size factors
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
estimating dispersions
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
gene-wise dispersion estimates
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
mean-dispersion relationship
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
final dispersion estimates
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
fitting model and testing
```


:::
:::


Extract our results:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
res <- results(dds)
head(res)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
log2 fold change (MLE): dex treated vs control 
Wald test p-value: dex treated vs control 
DataFrame with 6 rows and 6 columns

                  <numeric>      <numeric> <numeric> <numeric> <numeric>

ENSG00000000419  520.134160       0.206107  0.101042  2.039828 0.0413675
ENSG00000000457  322.664844       0.024527  0.145134  0.168996 0.8658000
ENSG00000000460   87.682625      -0.147143  0.256995 -0.572550 0.5669497
ENSG00000000938    0.319167      -1.732289  3.493601 -0.495846 0.6200029
ENSG00000000971 5760.148362       0.459282  0.234318  1.960081 0.0499864
```

:::
:::


## Volcano plot

A useful summary figure of our results is often called a volcano plot. It is basically a plot of log2 fold change values vs adjusted P-values

> Q. Use ggplot to make a first version "volcano plot" of `log2FoldChange` vs `padj`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(as.data.frame(res), aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = padj)) +
  geom_point()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: Removed 9917 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class13_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-16-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


This is not very useful because the y axis (p-values) is not really helpful - we want to focus on low p-values


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(as.data.frame(res), aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(padj))) +
  geom_point()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: Removed 9917 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class13_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-17-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


Adding some threshold lines:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(as.data.frame(res), aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(padj))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-2, 2)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: Removed 9917 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class13_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-18-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


## Add some plot annotation
> Q. Add color to the points (genes) we care about, nice axis labels, a useful title and a nice theme. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
res_df <- as.data.frame(res)

sig <- !is.na(res_df$padj) & (res_df$padj < 0.05)
up <- sig & (res_df$log2FoldChange > 2)
down <- sig & (res_df$log2FoldChange < -2)

res_df$group <- "not_sig"
res_df$group[up] <- "up"
res_df$group[down] <- "down"

ggplot(res_df, aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(padj), color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-2, 2)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05)) +
  labs(
    title = "Differential expression: dexamethasone vs control",
    x = "Log2 fold change",
    y = "-Log10 adjusted p-value"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: Removed 9917 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class13_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-19-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


## Save our results to a CSV file


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
write.csv(res, "results.csv")
```
:::



## Add Annotation Data
To make sense of our results we need to know what the differentially expressed genes are and what biological pathways and processes they are involved in. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(res)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
log2 fold change (MLE): dex treated vs control

ENSG00000000457  322.664844       0.024527  0.145134  0.168996 0.8658000

ENSG00000000971 5760.148362       0.459282  0.234318  1.960081 0.0499864
```

:::
:::


Lets start by mapping our ENSEMBLE ids to the more conventional gene SYMBOL. 
We will use two bioconductor packages for this mapping **AnnotationDbi** and **org.Hs.eg.db**.

We will first need to install these from bioconductor with `BiocManager::install("AnnotationDbi")`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(AnnotationDbi)
library(org.Hs.eg.db)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```

```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
columns(org.Hs.eg.db)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1] "ACCNUM"       "ALIAS"        "ENSEMBL"      "ENSEMBLPROT"  "ENSEMBLTRANS"
 [6] "ENTREZID"     "ENZYME"       "EVIDENCE"     "EVIDENCEALL"  "GENENAME"    
[11] "GENETYPE"     "GO"           "GOALL"        "IPI"          "MAP"         
[16] "OMIM"         "ONTOLOGY"     "ONTOLOGYALL"  "PATH"         "PFAM"        
[21] "PMID"         "PROSITE"      "REFSEQ"       "SYMBOL"       "UCSCKG"      
[26] "UNIPROT"     
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
res$symbol <- mapIds(org.Hs.eg.db,
                    keys = rownames(res), # ids
                    keytype = "ENSEMBL",# format
                    column = "SYMBOL") # Translation target
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns
```


:::
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
head(res)
```

> Q. Can you add "GENENAME" and "ENTREZID" as new columns to `res` named "name" and "entrez"

```{.r .cell-code}
                    keys = rownames(res), # our ids
                    keytype = "ENSEMBL",# their format

```

## Pathway analysis

Now we know the gene names (gene symbols) and their entrez IDs we can find out what pathways they are involved in. This is called "pathway analysis" or "gene set enrichment"

We will use the **gage** package and the **pathviewer** package to do this analysis (but there are loads of others)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(gage)
library(gageData)
library(pathview)
```
:::


Let's see what is in gageData, specifically KEGG pathways:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data(kegg.sets.hs)
head(kegg.sets.hs, 2)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$`hsa00232 Caffeine metabolism`
[1] "10"   "1544" "1548" "1549" "1553" "7498" "9"   

$`hsa00983 Drug metabolism - other enzymes`
 [1] "10"     "1066"   "10720"  "10941"  "151531" "1548"   "1549"   "1551"  
 [9] "1553"   "1576"   "1577"   "1806"   "1807"   "1890"   "221223" "2990"  
[17] "3251"   "3614"   "3615"   "3704"   "51733"  "54490"  "54575"  "54576" 
[25] "54577"  "54578"  "54579"  "54600"  "54657"  "54658"  "54659"  "54963" 
[33] "574537" "64816"  "7083"   "7084"   "7172"   "7363"   "7364"   "7365"  
[41] "7366"   "7367"   "7371"   "7372"   "7378"   "7498"   "79799"  "83549" 
[49] "8824"   "8833"   "9"      "978"   
```


:::
:::


To run our pathway analysis we will use the **gage()** function. It wants two main inputs: a vector of importance (in our case the log2 fold change values); and the gene sets to check overlap for.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
foldchanges <- res$log2FoldChange
names(foldchanges) <- res$symbol
head(foldchanges)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     TSPAN6        DPM1       SCYL3       FIRRM         FGR         CFH 
-0.35070296  0.20610728  0.02452701 -0.14714263 -1.73228897  0.45928190 
```


:::
:::


KEGG speaks entrez (ie uses ENTREZID format) not gene symbol format


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
names(foldchanges) <- res$entrez
keggres = gage(foldchanges, gsets=kegg.sets.hs)
head(keggres$less, 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
pathview(foldchanges, pathway.id = "hsa05310")
```
